title: Evidence of evolution
description: Test your knowledge on the evidence of evolution!

assessment_items.question_content:
The protein mTOR is a central regulator of cell growth and is highly conserved among vertebrates. Scientists compared the amino acid sequences of mTOR from five different vertebrate species, and recorded the number of amino acid differences in mTOR between each species pair. The results are shown in the table below.

>**Table 1.** Number of amino acid differences in mTOR for five species
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements is best supported by the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
|| **Number of amino acid differences in mTOR for five species** ||
 | Human | Zebrafish | Dog | Emperor penguin | Olive baboon
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
Human | $0$ | $265$ | $15$ | $114$ | $4$
Zebrafish |  | $0$ | $263$ | $244$ | $264$
Dog |  |  | $0$ | $117$ |  $15$
Emperor penguin |  |  |  | $0$ | $115$
Olive baboon |  |  |  |  | $0$

choices.content: Emperor penguins are more closely related to dogs than to zebrafish.
Olive baboons are more closely related to dogs than to humans.
Dogs are more closely related to emperor penguins than to humans.
Humans are more closely related to zebrafish than to emperor penguins.

assessment_items.question_content:
Many plants sense and respond to light with the help of the phytochrome A protein. The phytochrome A protein is encoded by the *PhyA* gene. 

Scientists compared the nucleotide sequences of the *PhyA* gene from five different plant species. To do this, the scientists calculated the percentage of nucleotides in the *PhyA* gene that were shared by each pair of species. The results are shown in the table below.

>**Table 1.** Percentage of identical nucleotides in *PhyA* coding sequences for five species
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements is best supported by the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
|| **Percentage of identical nucleotides in *PhyA* coding sequences for five species** ||
 | *C. sinensis* | *A. thaliana* | *G. max* | *C. rubella* | *A. halleri*
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
*C. sinensis* | $100\%$ | $76.5\%$ | $71.9\%$ | $76.5\%$ | $76.5\%$
*A. thaliana* |  | $100\%$ | $68.6\%$ | $94.8\%$ | $97.3\%$
*G. max* |  |  | $100\%$ | $68.2\%$ |  $68.8\%$
*C. rubella* |  |  |  | $100\%$ | $95.0\%$
*A. halleri* |  |  |  |  | $100\%$

choices.content: *A. thaliana* is more closely related to *C. sinensis* than to *G. max*.
*C. rubella* is more closely related to *G. max* than to *A. halleri*.
*A. halleri* is more closely related to *C. sinensis* than to *A. thaliana*.
*G. max* is more closely related to *A. halleri* than to *C. sinensis*.

assessment_items.question_content:
In order to study evolutionary relationships, a scientist compared the nucleotide sequences of Gene *X* in 5 different species. The number of nucleotide differences found between each species pair is summarized in the following table.

>**Table 1.** Number of nucleotide differences in Gene *X* for five species
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements is best supported by the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
|| **Number of nucleotide differences in Gene *X* for five species** ||
 | *Species A* | *Species B* | *Species C* | *Species D* | *Species E*
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
*Species A* | $0$ | $4$ | $4$ | $6$ | $10$
*Species B* |  | $0$ | $2$ | $8$ | $11$
*Species C* |  |  | $0$ | $7$ |  $11$
*Species D* |  |  |  | $0$ | $11$
*Species E* |  |  |  |  | $0$

choices.content: *Species D* is more closely related to *Species A* than to *Species E*.
*Species B* is more closely related to *Species D* than to *Species A*.
*Species C* is more closely related to *Species E* than to *Species B*.
*Species A* is more closely related to *Species D* than to *Species C*.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table indicates the presence or absence of selected traits for a set of animal species. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**The data about which trait provides the strongest evidence that all of the animal species in the table share a common ancestor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Data showing that a trait is present in *all* of the species in the table would provide the strongest evidence that the species share a common ancestor.
The data in the table shows that vertebrae are present in all of the species.

So, the correct answer is:

* vertebrae

options.alt:
|| **Presence (+) or absence (-) of traits in four species** ||
 | Mammary glands | Amniotic egg | Vertebrae | Gizzard | Feathers | Four limbs
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
Alligator | - | + | + | + | - | +
Chimpanzee | + | + | + | - | - | +
Tuna | - | - | + | - | - | -
Finch | - | + | + | + | + | +

choices.content: vertebrae
amniotic egg
gizzard
feathers

assessment_items.question_content:
All modern birds are descended from a group of dinosaurs called theropods. Theropods first appear in the fossil record during the late Triassic to early Jurassic period, roughly 200 million years ago. In contrast, fossils of taxa similar to modern birds can be found in the fossil record by the end of the Cretaceous period, roughly 66 million years ago.

The following table provides information about the presence of certain traits in theropods and modern birds. The presence of a trait is indicated by a checkmark.

Trait | Theropods | Modern birds
:-: | :-: | :-:
Bipedal | ✔ | ✔
Hollow bones | ✔ | ✔
Feathers |  | ✔
Long, bony tail | ✔ | 
Fused clavicle |  | ✔
Jaws with sharp teeth | ✔ | 
Reversed first toe |  |✔

**Each answer choice below describes a specific fossil. Which fossil provides the strongest evidence for the discovery of a transitional species between theropods and modern birds?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A fossil of a transitional species between theropods and modern birds would be found in a rock layer dating to sometime after the appearance of theropods in the fossil record, but before the appearance of modern birds. In addition, the fossil would have some features unique to theropods, and some features unique to modern birds.

According to the text, theropods appear in the fossil record around 200 million years ago, and modern birds are apparent in the fossil record by 66 million years ago. So, a fossil of a transitional species between theropods and modern birds would be found in a rock layer that is between 200 and 66 million years old.

A transitional species between theropods and modern birds would also have some traits that are unique to theropods, *and* some traits that are unique to modern birds. According to the table, the following traits are unique to theropods: a long, bony tail, and jaws with sharp teeth. The table also tells us that the following traits are unique to modern birds: feathers, fused clavicle, and a reversed front toe.

So, the fossil that provides the strongest evidence for the discovery of a transitional species between theropods and modern birds is:

* the fossil of an organism with feathers and a long, bony tail, found in a 145-million-year-old rock layer

choices.content: the fossil of an organism with feathers and a long, bony tail, found in a 145-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of an organism with fused clavicles and a reversed front toe, found in a 60-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of an organism that has jaws with sharp teeth and a long, bony tail, found in a 175-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of a bipedal organism with hollow bones, found in a 90-million-year-old rock layer

assessment_items.question_content:
All modern tetrapods (amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals) are descended from lobe-finned fish. Fossils of early lobe-finned fish can be found in rock layers that are at least 380 million years old. In contrast, true tetrapods first appear in the fossil record roughly 363 million years ago. 

The following table provides information about the presence of certain traits in early lobe-finned fish and tetrapods. The presence of a trait is indicated by a checkmark.

Trait | Early lobe-finned fish | Tetrapods
:-: | :-: | :-:
Bony skeleton | ✔ | ✔
Vertebrae | ✔ | ✔
Scaloid scales | ✔ | 
Limbs with digits |  | ✔
Fin rays | ✔ | 
Bending neck |  | ✔
Wrist bones |  |✔

**Each answer choice below describes a specific fossil. Which fossil provides the strongest evidence for the discovery of a transitional species between early lobe-finned fish and tetrapods?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A fossil of a transitional species between early lobe-finned fish and tetrapods would be found in a rock layer dating to sometime after the appearance of lobe-finned fish in the fossil record, but before the appearance of true tetrapods. In addition, the fossil would have some features unique to lobe-finned fish, and some features unique to tetrapods.

According to the text, lobe-finned fish can be found in rock layers that are at least 380 million years old, and true tetrapods first appear in the fossil record roughly 363 million years ago. So, a fossil of a transitional species between early lobe-finned fish and tetrapods would be found in a rock layer that is between 380 and 363 million years old.

A transitional species between early lobe-finned fish and tetrapods would also have some traits that are unique to lobe-finned fish, *and* some traits that are unique to tetrapods. According to the table, the following traits are unique to early lobe-finned fish: scaloid scales and fin rays. The table also tells us that the following traits are unique to tetrapods: limbs with digits, a bending neck, and wrist bones.

So, the fossil that provides the strongest evidence for the discovery of a transitional species between early lobe-finned fish and tetrapods is:

* the fossil of an organism with scaloid scales and a bending neck, found in a 375-million-year-old rock layer

choices.content: the fossil of an organism with scaloid scales and a bending neck, found in a 375-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of an organism with wrist bones and limbs with digits, found in a 345-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of an organism that has fin rays and scaloid scales, found in a 365-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of an organism with a bony skeleton and vertebrae, found in a 370-million-year-old rock layer

assessment_items.question_content:
Mammals are part of a group of organisms called synapsids. Synapsids are characterized by having a temporal fenestra, which is a hole in the skull behind the eye socket. 

All modern mammals are descended from early non-mammalian synapsids. Fossils of the earliest non-mammalian synapsids can be found in rock layers from roughly 305 million year ago. In contrast, true mammals first appear in the fossil record roughly 178 million years ago.

The following table provides information about the presence of certain traits in early non-mammalian synapsids and mammals. The presence of a trait is indicated by a checkmark.

Trait | Non-mammalian synapsids | Mammals
:-: | :-: | :-:
Temporal fenestra | ✔ | ✔
Hair |  | ✔
Lays eggs | ✔ | 
Sprawling gait | ✔ | 
Middle ear bones |  | ✔
Molars with full occlusion |  | ✔
Vertebrae | ✔ |✔

**Each answer choice below describes a specific fossil. Which fossil provides the strongest evidence for the discovery of a transitional species between early non-mammalian synapsids and mammals?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A fossil of a transitional species between early non-mammalian synapsids and mammals would be found in a rock layer dating to sometime after the appearance of non-mammalian synapsids in the fossil record, but before the appearance of true mammals. In addition, the fossil would have some features unique to non-mammalian synapsids, and some features unique to mammals.

According to the text, fossils of non-mammalian synapsids can be found in rock layers that are roughly 305 million years old, and fossils of true mammals appear in the fossil record roughly 178 million years ago. So, a fossil of a transitional species between early non-mammalian synapsids and mammals would likely be found in a rock layer that is between 305 and 178 million years old.

A transitional species between early non-mammalian synapsids and mammals would also have some traits that are unique to non-mammalian synapsids, *and* some traits that are unique to mammals. According to the table, the following traits are unique to early non-mammalian synapsids: lays eggs and has a sprawling gait. The table also tells us that the following traits are unique to mammals: hair, molars with full occlusion, and middle ear bones.

So, the fossil that provides the strongest evidence for the discovery of a transitional species between early non-mammalian synapsids and mammals is:

* the fossil of an organism with a sprawling gait and middle ear bones, found in a 250-million-year-old rock layer

choices.content: the fossil of an organism with a sprawling gait and middle ear bones, found in a 250-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of an organism with hair and molars with full occlusion, found in a 160-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of an organism that lays eggs and has a sprawling gait, found in a 275-million-year-old rock layer
the fossil of an organism with a temporal fenestra and vertebrae, found in a 300-million-year-old rock layer

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements support the hypothesis that dolphins are more closely related to humans than to fish?**

👁️ **Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Dolphins and humans have forelimbs with a similar bone structure, while fish do not.
Dolphins and fish have more nucleotide differences in their ribosomal RNA gene sequences than dolphins and humans do.
Dolphins and humans have gill slits during embryonic stages of development, and so do fish.
Dolphins and fish have fins for swimming in aquatic environments, while humans have legs for walking on land.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements support the hypothesis that crocodiles are more closely related to birds than to frogs?**

👁️ **Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Crocodiles and birds have eggs that are protected by an amniotic membrane, while frogs do not.
Crocodiles and frogs have more nucleotide differences in their homologous DNA sequences than crocodiles and birds do.
Crocodiles and birds have four limbs, and so do frogs.
Crocodile and frog forelimbs are adapted for walking, while bird forelimbs are adapted for flight.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements support the hypothesis that bats are more closely related to gorillas than to birds?**

👁️ **Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Bats and gorillas have one hole behind each eye socket in the skull, while birds have two.
Bats and birds have more nucleotide differences in their mitochondrial DNA sequences than bats and gorillas do.
Bats and gorillas have four limbs with digits, and so do birds.
Bats and birds have wings that allow flight, while gorillas do not.
